data.title: China’s clinical trial capacity matches US, attracts more global pharma firms, investors
data.description:
China’s growing pipeline of novel drug candidates is expected to draw increased attention from global pharmaceutical companies and investors scouting for licensing opportunities, as the country’s clinical trial capacity has expanded to match those in the US.
That assessment was made by Shanghai-based Helen Chen, Greater China managing partner at L.E.K. Consulting, one of the most respected analysts in the mainland’s life sciences industry.
“There is increased interest from multinational corporations in conducting investigator-initiated trials for cell and gene therapies in China, given that they can have access to patient data more rapidly than in other markets,” Chen said.
Such trials refer to clinical studies led by academic researchers or physicians – instead of pharmaceutical companies – often to explore new uses for treatments.
As of 2024, China’s clinical trial volumes matched 80 per cent of those in the US and exceeded the levels in Europe by 10 per cent, according to a report jointly published last week by L.E.K. Consulting and PharmaDJ.
The report, which polled 89 pharmaceutical and contract research organisation leaders between August and September, showed China had 2,694 new clinical trials in 2024, up 13 per cent from a year earlier.
Multinational companies accounted for 14.3 per cent of those clinical trials in the country last year, representing a 7 per cent compound annual growth rate since 2019. Most of the trials in China were launched by domestic pharmaceutical firms, some of which have global drug ambitions.
“We don’t see multinational firms slowing down their clinical trials in China due to geopolitical tensions,” Chen said. “As the second largest pharmaceutical market globally, China remains an important market for these multinational companies.”

“The renewed growth of innovative clinical trials suggests that the pipeline of biotech firms is getting more robust,” she added.
These developments reflected China’s push towards greater biomedical manufacturing as part of technological self-reliance.
Biomedicine was identified by Beijing as a key pillar to enhance domestic industrial supply chains, forming part of the country’s next five-year development blueprint, according to a JPMorgan report dated October 29.
In the coming years, the investment bank expected Chinese policymakers to provide more targeted support for research and development and commercialisation in the biomedical manufacturing sector.
Meanwhile, Chen pointed out that the larger number of clinical studies in China translated to “more assets to be discovered by pharma search teams for licensing”, as well as more opportunities for investors to set up new companies on the mainland.
Many major deals between multinational firms and domestic biotech companies “are built on China-only data”, said Dai Jialing Dai, PharmaDJ’s president and publisher, in a statement. He asserted that China would remain an important market for clinical development for both global pharmaceutical firms and domestic biotech companies.
Chinese pharmaceutical companies’ sale of early-stage assets to global partners soared to nearly US$70 billion as of September 2025, an eightfold increase from the level of transactions in 2021, according to the L.E.K. Consulting and PharmaDJ report.
Over the past five years, China’s out-licensing deals have expanded into a wider range of therapeutic modalities, according to the report. These mostly focus on cancer treatments such as “guided missile-like” chemotherapy antibody-drug conjugates, bispecific antibodies that target two disease pathways and cell therapies in which living cells are engineered to fight or repair disease.
Total research spending by China’s top 50 biopharmaceutical companies by revenue reached US$8.5 billion last year, which marked a 12 per cent compound annual growth rate since 2021, the report said.
“This could mean a substantive upgrade on the importance of biotech or pharma companies,” the report said.

data.title: Israeli air strikes kill 25 in Gaza, rattling ceasefire
data.description:
At least 25 Palestinians were killed in four Israeli air strikes on Wednesday in a part of Gaza under Hamas control since a shaky ceasefire took effect in October, health authorities said.
The Israeli military said its forces struck Hamas targets across Gaza after members of the Palestinian militant group fired on its troops in violation of the nearly six-week-old ceasefire. No Israeli forces were injured.
Hamas condemned the Israeli strikes as a dangerous escalation, and urged the United States to “honour its stated commitments and exert immediate pressure on Israel to enforce the ceasefire and halt its attacks”.
But a US official, who spoke anonymously, said Hamas was aiming to break the ceasefire and not fulfil its commitment to demilitarise.
“These desperate tactics will fail,” the official said.

Medical workers said 10 people were killed in the Gaza City suburb of Zeitoun, two in the Shejaia suburb to the east, and the rest in two separate attacks in Khan Younis in the southern Gaza Strip.
Repeated shooting incidents have pointed to the fragility of the ceasefire. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for what both call violations of the US-brokered truce, the first stage of President Donald Trump’s 20-point plan for a post-war Gaza.
All four attacks were far beyond an agreed-upon imaginary “yellow line” separating the areas under Israeli and Palestinian control, according to medical staff, witnesses and Palestinian media.
The Zeitoun attack was on a building belonging to Muslim religious authorities and the Khan Younis attack was on a UN-run club, both of which house displaced families.
The October 10 ceasefire in the two-year Gaza war has eased the conflict, enabling hundreds of thousands of Palestinians to return to Gaza’s ruins. Israel has pulled troops back from city positions, and aid flows have increased.
But violence has not completely halted. Palestinian health authorities say Israeli forces have killed 305 people in strikes on Gaza since the truce, nearly half of them in one day last week when Israel retaliated for an attack on its troops.
Israel says three of its soldiers have been killed since the ceasefire began and it has targeted scores of fighters.

data.title: China’s bond market gains as central bank resumes debt purchases amid growth concerns
data.description:
China’s bond market is set to gain further momentum, buoyed by the central bank’s resumption of government debt purchases, as softer economic data and downward pressure on growth necessitate further policy easing, according to analysts.
Huayuan Securities forecast that the upward trend was likely to drive the yield on China’s benchmark 10-year sovereign bond down to 1.65 per cent within this year. Southwest Securities predicted a drop to 1.7 per cent. The yield currently stands at 1.808 per cent.
Economic data for October indicated a worsening outlook for growth, bolstering the case for monetary easing. Industrial production grew less than expected, fixed-asset investment shrank, retail sales slowed for a fifth consecutive month, and declines in housing prices showed no signs of abating.
Expectations for policy loosening in China have also strengthened amid hopes for more rate cuts in the US, which would align the monetary policies of the world’s two largest economies to mitigate foreign exchange volatility.
“Given the weak economic data and falling property prices, there is an increasing urgency for the policy rate to fall,” said Liao Zhiming, an analyst at Huayuan Securities. The firm anticipated a 20-basis-point cut in the policy rate in the next six months, which would support the bond market in the fourth quarter.
“We are positive on bonds,” Liao said.

China’s bonds had underperformed relative to stocks until recently, as enthusiasm for artificial intelligence and improved Sino-US relations drove investors towards riskier assets.
The yield on 10-year debt rose to as high as 1.905 per cent in September, increasing by 28 basis points over four months. However, the trend reversed last month when the People’s Bank of China resumed buying government bonds after a nine-month hiatus, injecting more liquidity into the market.
ING suggested that Beijing might avoid significant stimulus this year to preserve policy tools, as the annual growth target of about 5 per cent appeared attainable this year. Still, the need for strengthened monetary and fiscal support for the economy was intensifying following the November data release, the Dutch bank said.
Nomura Holdings expected a 10-basis-point cut in borrowing costs by year-end to stimulate short-term growth.
Investor sentiment is shifting in favour of bonds, as the stock rally shows signs of fatigue amid concerns about inflated technology stock valuations and excessive investment in AI. A benchmark tracking the 50 largest stocks on the tech-heavy Star Market in Shanghai has fallen over 10 per cent in the past month.
Xiangcai Securities recommended that investors adopt a dip-buying strategy for bond investments, with the yield on the 10-year bond expected to fluctuate between 1.75 per cent and 1.85 per cent in the near term.
China’s bond market saw its largest bull run in a decade last year, as investors flocked to safe-haven assets in response to a rapidly deteriorating growth outlook. Strong demand pushed the yield on government debt maturing in 10 years down by 88.5 basis points in 2024, prompting a warning from the central bank that gains had been too rapid.
“The economic data is weakening and market expectations for loosening are building,” said Yang Jiefeng, an analyst at Southwest Securities. “That’ll boost sentiment for long positions in bonds as the year comes to a close.”

data.title: Male vs female hairy crabs: which are tastiest? Hong Kong chefs peel back the differences
data.description:
Autumn signals the start of the hairy crab season in Hong Kong and parts of mainland China.
A question that divides hairy crab lovers is: are males or females the most delicious?
When it comes to the flavour of the crabs, seasonality matters a lot. Male crabs hit their peak in the 10th lunar month, when they develop plump, smooth gonads. The 11th lunar month is the female crabs’ time to shine as their roe ripens and becomes firmer.
Four Hong Kong chefs compare male and female hairy crabs further to help us understand what makes each desirable.

Junno Li, executive Chinese chef at The Chinese Library in Hong Kong’s Tai Kwun centre of heritage and culture, says that female crabs are prized for their rich-tasting roe.
“The female crab roe is rich, creamy and has a deep golden colour. The flavour is intense and buttery, making it perfect if you like something more robust,” he says.
“Male crabs, on the other hand, do not have roe; instead, they have white, jellylike reproductive glands that are incredibly silky and delicate on the palate, and the taste is much lighter and more subtle.”
Menex Cheung, executive chef of China Tang in the Landmark in Central, echoes that a key difference between male and female crabs is the richness of the females’ roe.
“Male crabs have creamy [reproductive glands] … while female crabs have yellow crab roe and are known for their rich crab roe aroma,” he says.

Another key difference is the meat texture.
“Male crabs are generally larger in size and therefore have relatively more plump meat in their crab claws and feet,” says Cheung Kin-ming, executive chef of two-Michelin-star Ying Jee Club.
He adds that the meat from both males and females is fresh and sweet. “But the point of tasting crab is always its roe and accessory glands.”
Why do hairy crabs tend to get more attention than other types of seasonal crustaceans? Silas Li, executive chef of Hong Kong Cuisine 1983 in Happy Valley, feels that there is a certain prestige attached to the act of eating hairy crabs.
“It’s the luxury of eating this rare delicacy. The flavour is so intense and the texture is so smooth. There is also a lot of labour involved when you’re eating [them] at a restaurant. It takes the chefs hours to deshell the crabs and turn them into a dish,” he says.

Menex Cheung feels that the female crab roe holds more cultural value. “The crab roe represents abundance and preciousness, as the crab roe is treated as the most delicious and precious part of the crab. Its richness and delicate flavour are the best showcase of Chinese gastronomy,” he says.
Cheung Kin-ming feels it is about seasonality.
“First, this embodies the philosophy of eating within the seasons, and savouring hairy crabs is a way to appreciate the unique richness of autumn,” he says.
“Second, Chinese culinary culture places great emphasis on the texture of ingredients. The powdery and glutinous texture of crab roe and the rich and sticky texture of crab accessory glands offer a unique taste experience that is difficult to match with other ingredients.”

For those new to hairy crab, most chefs recommend starting with the more punchy-flavoured females.
“You can really taste the richness with female crabs,” Silas Li says. “The sticky pastiness of the male may take some getting used to, but the strong flavours of the female are something that stand out.”
However, Menex Cheung suggests first-timers eat male crabs as he feels the flavour of the females could be too strong for the uninitiated.
“I usually recommend male crabs for their plump and rich meat, [which makes it easier for] newcomers to appreciate the crabs’ freshness and creaminess. Female crabs might be too greasy for a first-timer due to their oiliness and richness of crab roe,” he says.

The chefs have different ideas on how to contrast the flavours of male and female hairy crabs in a multi-course set.
“In a multi-course crab menu, I’d design it so the two crabs play different roles,” Junno Li says.
“The male crab’s clean, silky reproductive glands and the female crab’s rich, buttery roe each get their moment, prepared with different techniques to highlight what makes them special.
“You’d have texture contrasts, the thickness of roe against the silkiness of male glands, as well as flavour contrasts, with the richness of female against the fresh sweetness of male. By layering all these contrasts, diners get the full spectrum of what each crab offers.”

Menex Cheung places importance on the order in which the crabs are eaten.
“I suggest having male crabs to begin the meal. The freshness and natural sweetness of crabmeat would be a good beginning of the meal, and enjoying the rich and delicate female crab roe afterwards fully showcases hairy crabs’ distinctive tastes and multifaceted allure,” he says.
Silas Li feels that things might not have to be so polarising.
“There are certain dishes like dumplings and toppings where the female roe is better, and the richness of the male is better used in mains, but I like to mix them together. In the same bite, you can enjoy the best of both male and female crab – the strong flavour of the females and then the rich texture of the male,” he says.

Cheung Kin-ming has a more methodical approach.
“For the appetiser, crab roe from female crabs can be used to make crab roe soup dumplings and other dim sum. Then the crabs can be presented whole and steamed to allow guests to savour the cleanest flavour from both male and female crabs,” he says.
“Then, the roe from male crabs is used to make rice or noodles, utilising its rich and oily qualities to create a memorable experience for the crab feast.
“Finally, the crab shells can be simmered into a delicious soup, blending the flavours of both male and female crabs, providing a perfect ending.”
Whichever you prefer, Junno Li says that hairy crabs are more than just something delicious to eat.
“They represent the essence of the season, the pinnacle of what an ingredient can offer, and a kind of respect for nature’s rhythms.”

data.title: US and China should cooperate to make space safe for all
data.description:
Three Chinese astronauts have returned home safely from the Tiangong space station after a delay of more than a week. Their scheduled return journey was disrupted when tiny cracks appeared in the porthole of their homeward-bound capsule. The damage was likely to have been caused by space debris. The astronauts were lucky in that there was a replacement backup spacecraft; otherwise, they might have been stranded for much longer. Two American astronauts recently had to put up with an extra nine months at the International Space Station because of spacecraft malfunctions.
The Tiangong episode, which fortunately was quickly resolved, is a reminder of the growing danger of orbiting space junk. An intensifying race between spacefaring nations and the rapid commercialisation of space means the problem, which could one day cause fatalities, will only get worse unless governments and companies agree to cooperate to address it.
The next generation of telecommunications, the increasing reliance of online connections on orbiting networks of small satellites and advanced spacecraft carrying professional astronauts – and eventually space tourists – all make space missions and travel increasingly busy and therefore more dangerous. Tiny bits of space debris could have catastrophic consequences. It’s a disaster waiting to happen.
When it comes to the space race, there is virtually no cooperation between the two main competitors, China and the United States. The International Space Station allows Russians on board but not Chinese. Space safety is one area where Chinese, Americans and everyone else can benefit from cooperation, coordination and the sharing of information and costs. A global network is currently tracking thousands of pieces of junk clogging up low Earth orbit.
New technology is being developed that could knock some low-orbiting junk into the atmosphere so it can safely burn out on descent. A global warning system, shared rescue and repair procedures, a common code of conduct and international treaties and protocols – just like those for today’s sea travels, which were once also hazardous – need to be in place for everyone’s protection.

data.title: National Games: Hong Kong fencers go again, Fan v Wang part 2 – what to watch on day 11
data.description:
We’re much closer to the end than the beginning of what has been an enthralling National Games, but we’re not done yet.
Fresh off a first fencing gold for the men’s foil team, their épée counterparts get a chance to redeem themselves after the individual slip-ups they all suffered earlier this week.
In badminton, mixed doubles Olympic champions Zheng Siwei and Huang Yaqiong are proving that age is just a number. Having retired after their golden hurrah in Paris, they came back for an encore and are one win away from another title.
Four wheels might be good, but two are better where Hong Kong’s athletes are concerned, and there is a chance for more gold in mountain biking.
Here’s what is coming up on the penultimate day of the 15th National Games.

Men’s épée redemption hour
The city’s men’s épée team are looking to shine as a unit after suffering early exits in the individual event earlier this week.
Cedric Ho Wai-hang and Anson Ng Ho-tin, the 2024 Asian champion and runner-up respectively, and Fong Hoi-sun, who won National Games bronze in 2017, fancy their chances as a collective, despite none getting past the second round individually on Monday.
Hong Kong may be handed a lower seeding as a consequence, but Ho said he was determined to “bring down” whomever they face.
The women’s foil team are also in action.
Titans in action again
We reach the last event at the Galaxy Arena, and there is arguably nothing better than the table tennis men’s team final.
Quality is almost guaranteed, given the stellar names, as Shanghai and Beijing go head to head from 6pm.
Olympic champion Fan Zhendong, representing Shanghai for the first time at the Games, has already this week pocketed the men’s singles gold, ahead of world No 2 Lin Shidong and world champion Wang Chuqin.
Expected to play in two singles matches, Fan also has doubles specialist Xu Xin by his side. Beijing will have world No 1 Wang and the legendary Ma Long.
Taking on the professionals
Hong Kong’s baseball players will fight Tianjin for seventh spot from 9am, after Shandong condemned them to their second-straight single-run loss in Zhongshan on Wednesday.
The city team, who lost 5-4 to Fujian in their closing group fixture, were beaten 7-6 by Shandong in their fifth-to-eighth semi-final, after leading 5-4 following the third inning.
Outfielder Ken Yung Chun-wai said the result was “truly a shame” and his side felt “this was a match we absolutely could have won”, despite Shandong being stocked with professional players.
“We hope to absorb this valuable experience and allow it to forge us into a better Hong Kong team for future matches,” Yung said.
Tianjin also boast a team of professionals but Yung said: “We will put forward our absolute maximum effort to earn the win.”
Pair’s final bite?
The return of mixed-doubles badminton duo Zheng Siwei and Huang Yaqiong just for the National Games seems to have been worthwhile, with the pair making it to the final.
Zheng and Huang, who are representing Zhejiang, retired from the sport after winning gold at the Paris Olympics.

And they have been relatively untroubled, receiving a first-round bye and then not having to finish their quarter-final after Fujian’s Ou Xuanyi and Huang Dongping retired in the third set.
They then beat Guangdong’s Gao Jiaxuan and Chen Fanghui to reach the final.
At the Shenzhen Gymnasium decider, the seasoned duo will face the younger Hunan pair of Zhou Zhihong and Wei Yaxin.
Mountain bike time
If you thought Ceci Lee Sze-wing had drawn a line under the action on two wheels when she won her third gold of these National Games on Monday, you have another think coming.
Hong Kong still have three cyclists chasing honours today, this time in the mountain bike competition over in Jiangmen.
It is an early start for Gary Lai Chun-kin, who will go in the men’s competition from 9am.
The women begin at 11am, with Hong Kong represented by Heidi Yu Hoi-tik and the versatile Kwan Tsz-kwan, who helped Lee win road race gold last week and was also a member of the city’s team pursuit quartet in the city’s Velodrome.

data.title: HKUST medical school can make a difference as the population ages
data.description:
The arguments for Hong Kong having a third medical school – from a shortage of doctors to the importance of education and research to the city’s future as a hub and superconnector – left no real alternative. The challenge for a third school, according to Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu in his policy address last year, is “innovative strategic positioning” that complements the schools run by the University of Hong Kong and the Chinese University of Hong Kong.
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology has won the right to operate it on the recommendation of a special task group including the secretaries of health and education. Its bid won out over proposals from Polytechnic and Baptist universities.
Secretary for Health Lo Chung-mau said the group chose HKUST on the basis of 10 criteria, including clear strategic positioning and broad global vision. “We think that it would have a winning edge in terms of competition and attraction in recruiting professorial manpower and students,” Lo said. “Its position also combined medicine and research and development into one.”
HKUST had strong advantages in life sciences, artificial intelligence and big data, and Lo said he hoped it could apply these in the development of the medical school. Financially, he said HKUST put up around the same amount as the government for medical school operations and infrastructure for the next 25 years.
Patients who have waited for hours to see a doctor at public hospitals – or months to years in some cases, for appointments to see specialists – might wonder what difference it will all make, especially if they read projections that Hong Kong will suffer a shortage of 1,570 doctors by 2030 and 1,200 by 2040. Moreover, only about half the city’s registered doctors work in the public system.
HKUST plans an initial intake of 50 students for a four-year course at its Clear Water Bay campus. That seems unlikely to make much difference to an ageing population, even if the shortages turn out to be smaller.
However, technology and a move to its own teaching hospital in the Northern Metropolis University Town around 2034 could make a difference to accessibility. The university said yesterday it had already recruited six clinical professors and would commit more than HK$7 billion to the project.
Hong Kong is not alone in coming to grips with adapting health systems for the needs of an ageing population. A third medical school that promotes medical innovation and scientific research will enhance the city’s global competitiveness and foster partnerships, in addition to using its northern location to collaborate with the rest of the Greater Bay Area.

data.title: US lawmakers press for new tactics to challenge China’s grip on rare earths
data.description:
US lawmakers on Wednesday called for a change in Washington’s strategy to evade China’s chokehold on rare earth mineral supplies, arguing that new approaches are needed in parallel with President Donald Trump’s efforts to speed up domestic production.
Proposals to dismantle the chokepoint – including new technologies and development of components that do not require the critical minerals as inputs – were presented during a hearing of the US House Select Committee on China.
The suggestions came as frustration builds over the slow pace of US mining and processing and China’s de facto control of global prices. New technologies that could replace rare earths in critical components, however, remain years away.
“We’re chasing tails. I don’t think we’re ever gonna get to the point that we’re gonna be able to compete with the Chinese on the rare earth issue, I really don’t,” Carlos Gimenez, a Republican US congressman for Florida, said at the hearing.
“I think the Chinese are so far ahead, it’s impossible for us to catch up.”


Executives from three major US mineral companies – rare earth miner MP Materials, Lithium Americas and Niron Magnetics – were invited to testify at the hearing, which focused on China’s dominance in global pricing power and possible US responses.
All three firms received funding from the US government, including grants or equity investment. The US Department of Defence invested US$400 billion in July in MP Materials to get a 15 per cent stake.
However, corporate executives gave no clear answer to how long the US can shift away from Chinese supplies, particularly against the backdrop of the recently reached one-year truce.
“I just think that if we even try, they’ll take steps to make sure that it fails, fill that embargo us or whatever. And then we’ll cry uncle and that’s it,” Gimenez said, calling for more attention on investing in alternatives.
The public hearing came at a time when policymakers in Washington have amplified their concerns over reliance on Chinese supplies, particularly after the rare earth export control earlier this year, and it also reflects a division on strategy – whether the US should go full blast on developing alternatives or just try to catch the Chinese at the rare earth game.
China produces the vast majority of the world’s rare earths – essential for electric vehicles, electronics and defence components – giving Beijing leverage as Trump’s global tariff war, announced in April, unfolds.
The two countries reached a tariff truce and export control pause after the South Korean summit between Chinese President Xi Jinping and Trump in late October. US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said earlier this week that a rare earths deal between the US and China will “hopefully” be done by Thanksgiving, which falls on November 27.
Raja Krishnamoorthi, ranking member of the China committee and also a Democrat representative for Illinois, said “it’s time for a Manhattan Project-like effort” to end US rare earth dependency on China.
“Mining and processing more rare earths in America is only one part of the solution, I respectfully submit. We also need alternatives that potentially bypass rare earths entirely,” he said at the hearing.

Krishnamoorthi also presented a picture of Jian-Ping Wang, a University of Minnesota professor who develops a technology for replacing rare earths. “If we want to break China’s stranglehold on magnets, we need an all hands on deck approach. That means welcoming the best talent from around the world, including China,” he said.
The Democratic congressman’s remark appeared to allude to heightened Republican scrutiny of Chinese researchers and students in the US academic system over national security concerns – a climate that analysts say may be encouraging more Chinese scientists to return home.
For Minnesota-based Niron Magnetics, which is using an entirely new technology called iron nitride to replace rare earths in magnet production, it still needs time and more funding to scale up production.
“It is not gonna be quick and easy,” said Jonathan Rowntree, the company’s CEO.
“We’re fortunate that for now [we’re in] a place where we’ve already gone from the gram to the ton scale.”

data.title: China’s soybean revolution: the change that rewrote global trade and tested food security
data.description:
Soybeans are more than just a crop – they have become a litmus test of Beijing’s evolving food security strategy and sit at the heart of the US-China trade war.
As a crucial source of animal feed and cooking oil, soybeans have remained a flashpoint throughout the ebbs and flows of the two nations’ trade disputes, from the tariff war during US President Donald Trump’s first term to the current one.
Less well known is China’s transformation from soybean exporter to the world’s leading importer. Spanning three decades, researchers believe the shift reflects evolving domestic needs and the impact of policy reforms, international agreements and technological innovations aimed at bolstering food security.
Today, China imports about 100 million tonnes a year – about 60 per cent of global trade in the commodity – an evolution that has reshaped international markets and farmers' livelihoods.
In proposals released in late October for the 15th five-year plan, Beijing called for efforts to “diversify agricultural imports and better align trade with domestic production” – underscoring the country’s persistent impact on one of the world’s most traded commodities.
“It seems necessary for China to be self-reliant on soybeans [given current geopolitical risks], but it’s impossible to actually achieve this,” said Zheng Fengtian, a professor from Renmin University in Beijing who has studied Chinese agriculture for over two decades.
The country’s limited arable land cannot accommodate the crop at the scale required, he said, while attempting to produce all soybeans domestically would divert land away from critical staple grains – ultimately threatening food security.
From exporter to world’s top importer
The roots of Beijing’s dilemma can be traced back to the early 1990s, when China was a net exporter of soybeans – a crop believed to have been domesticated in the country over 6,000 years ago. According to the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, output peaked at 16 million tonnes in 1994, driven by large-scale cultivation in fertile, black soil-rich northeastern regions such as Heilongjiang province.
The prevailing logic in China was that soybean imports brought more benefits than drawbacks
Zheng Fengtian, Renmin University
Exports routinely exceeded 1 million tonnes a year, with official data from the US Department of Agriculture (USDA) showing that China retained its status as an exporter until 1995 by shipping surpluses to regional markets.
But the landscape soon shifted dramatically. By 2000, imports had surged to over 10 million tonnes, reversing the trade balance into a persistent deficit. China’s integration into the global trade system and soaring domestic demand – fuelled by rapid urbanisation and rising middle-class affluence – drove the trend, Zheng said.
“The United States, which wields significant control over the global food market, emerged as a key enabler in this import surge, particularly following China’s World Trade Organization (WTO) accession in December 2001,” he added.
Before China joined the WTO, US soybean shipments to the country averaged below 1 million tonnes a year. But under the 1999 US-China Agricultural Cooperation Agreement and the bilateral WTO accession protocol, Beijing agreed to cut agricultural tariffs to an average of 17 per cent. For soybeans, duties would be slashed to 3 per cent and quotas and licensing restrictions were gradually removed.
With domestic demand soaring and strong US production, soybeans – along with wheat, cotton and meat – were prioritised in the accords, expanding market access for American exports.
Liberalisation catapulted US deliveries to 32 million tonnes by 2016, capturing 38 per cent of China’s import share at its zenith. American advantages – including yields almost double those of China’s, genetically modified strains and streamlined logistics – powered the boom and supported China’s crushing industry.
“It was, and still is, a principle for China to remain self-sufficient for staple grains – rice, wheat, corn – and since soybean yields were low, we opened up the sector at the time,” Zheng said.
“From WTO accession until 2018, the prevailing logic in China was that soybean imports brought more benefits than drawbacks – they effectively spared our arable land for more important crops.”


In the seven years leading up to the 2018 trade war, the US exported an average of 28 per cent of its soybeans to China. That share peaked at 31 per cent in the 2020 to 2021 marketing year following the phase one trade agreement, according to the American Soybean Association.
China’s domestic market has also undergone sweeping changes in recent decades, turning soybeans from a modest staple into a critical input for the food industry. Meat consumption exploded as living standards rose, fuelling an insatiable demand for soybean meal as the country’s primary livestock feed. Vegetable oil consumption also surged, with soybean oil’s affordability making it one of the most widely consumed.
On the supply side, limited farmland and a huge population forced policymakers to prioritise essentials like rice and wheat, reallocating arable land and relegating soybean farming to secondary status.
“As soybean yields are low, which means lower profitability than other crops, farmers are not keen to grow it,” said Zhong Yu, a researcher at the Institute of Agricultural Economics and Development at the China Academy of Agricultural Sciences.
“And China’s relatively small size of arable land, compared to its 1.4 billion population, is the biggest constraint, necessitating a strategy to balance domestic and international markets.”
Trade war spurs policy shift
Fears mounted in 2018 during Trump’s first term, prompting Beijing to reassess its dependence on American crops. “People began to realise that the US was turning China into an enemy to curb its rise, and might weaponise soybeans – though today it’s also a weapon for China in trade talks with the US,” Zheng said.
To counter this vulnerability, Beijing pushed to refine agricultural policy, notably through the annual No 1 Document – the first directive issued by the Communist Party’s Central Committee each year, traditionally dedicated to agriculture and rural affairs.
The 2019 version introduced a soybean revitalisation plan that called for expanded planting through diverse methods. The strategy has remained an important theme of agricultural policy, with substantial investments poured into breeding higher performing varieties.




Production rose 29 per cent from 16 million tonnes in 2018 to 20.65 million tonnes last year, according to China’s National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) – though this covered only about 20 per cent of the country’s huge import demand.
While per-unit yields remain below 60 per cent of those grown in the US, according to figures from the NBS and the USDA, China has invested heavily in breeding high-yield, high-oil varieties and has achieved some success in trials.
In September, the official Farmers’ Daily reported that scientists had developed several new varieties with oil content exceeding 22 per cent and regional trial yields 8 per cent higher than regular strains. These advances “will boost soybean oilseed yields, increase product value for oil processors, drive industry upgrades, reduce import reliance and strengthen China’s food security”, according to the report.
China has also diversified its suppliers, increasing imports from Brazil and Argentina while investing in infrastructure to facilitate trade. Recent customs data showed that Brazilian soybeans continued to dominate in September, with a nearly 30 per cent year-on-year increase, accounting for 85.5 per cent of China’s imports for the month.
Looking ahead: a push for alternatives
Soybean imports could fall to about 79 million tonnes by 2034 as domestic capabilities strengthen, according to the China Agricultural Outlook Report for 2025 to 2034, published by an expert committee under the agriculture ministry in April.
That trajectory aligns with the leadership’s pledge in this year’s No 1 Document to “consolidate” achievements in soybean expansion, signalling a shift from raising production to stabilising it.
“China will still need the international market for supplies, but imports can be reduced through substitution and reduced consumption,” Zhong said, noting that alternative proteins could replace soybeans in livestock feed.
“Also, edible oil consumption has now become somewhat excessive after decades of upgrades to nutritional structure, leaving room for health-driven cutbacks.”
Under President Xi Jinping’s “all-encompassing approach to food” to guarantee security, Beijing has pursued replacements beyond traditional crops. The State Council issued a directive last year to establish a diversified food supply system by 2027, with alternatives expected to come from forest-based food industries, grass-fed livestock and deep-sea aquaculture.

data.title: Many young South Koreans back later retirement amid pension doubts
data.description:
Kim Sung-ho, 38, an office worker, said he is increasingly open to the idea of raising the retirement age in South Korea.
“A few years ago, I opposed extending the retirement age,” he said. “But now, living costs and housing prices are getting higher. I worry more about my own future. I think I will need a stable source of income until I get older. Extending retirement age isn’t about the older generation any more. It’s about preparing for the future for my generation too.”
Similarly, An Ju-young, a marketing assistant, 34, said income from being employed as a regular salaried worker is better than relying on the state-run pension payout.
“My generation doesn’t trust the pension system. I think I speak for my generation when I say we don’t believe the national pension will support us by the time we get to the retirement age, given South Korea’s superaged population and all that,” she said.
If retirement age is pushed back, then we would have more years to save for post-retirement life
An Ju-young, marketing assistant
“That’s the hard truth and everyone knows it. If retirement age is pushed back, then at least we would have more years to earn monthly income and save for post-retirement life. It’s more practical.”
Kim and An are among many younger South Koreans increasingly supporting the government drive to extend the retirement age to 65 from the current 60.
It marks a significant shift from what was previously understood as a generational conflict between older workers wanting more years at the cost of reduced job opportunities for younger people.
According to a survey by Remember & Company, 74 per cent of 1,037 office workers said the retirement age should be extended.

Most noteworthy was that 7 out of 10 workers in the 20–39 age group voiced support.
The survey said 67.9 per cent of those in their 20s agreed, while the support rate was 70.4 per cent among those in their 30s.
Nearly 4 in 5, or 77.9 per cent in their 50s gave the same response, while the support rate was higher at 80.8 per cent among those in their 60s.
Only 12.9 per cent favoured the status quo. About 13 per cent said they would prefer abolishing the retirement age altogether.
When asked why extension was necessary, more than a third, or 39 per cent, said they would want stability after retirement.


Those aged 60 and older said they would want their decades of experience to be of better use.
Remember & Company said the results show that young workers no longer consider retirement extension as a threat, but rather as something they need with their own future uncertainties factored in.
“An increasing number of younger generations are seeing this issue differently. Korea’s overall human resources and employment management need to adjust goals towards longer working lives,” it said.
This article was first published by The Korea Times
